title: Handball Not Given — Denied Clear Penalty
summary: A clear handball in the box by a defender was missed by both the referee and VAR, denying a clear goalscoring opportunity.
description: In the World Cup Final, with France leading 1–0, a Croatian attacker's shot struck the arm of a French defender inside the penalty area. The defender's arm was in an unnatural position, away from the body, and clearly made the silhouette larger. Despite the incident occurring in full view of the referee and being subject to VAR review, neither the on-field referee nor the VAR team deemed it a penalty. While VAR was in use for the 2018 World Cup, the threshold for intervention was set very high, and this incident was controversially deemed not a 'clear and obvious error.' The decision had significant implications given the match was the World Cup Final and the score remained 1–0.

images.caption: The defender's arm was clearly away from the body when struck by the ball.

rules.explanation: Law 12 explicitly states that handball is an offence when a player 'deliberately touches the ball with their hand/arm' or when they 'touch the ball with their hand/arm when it has made their body unnaturally bigger.' A player is considered to have made their body unnaturally bigger when the position of their hand/arm is not a consequence of, or justifiable by, the player's body movement for that specific situation. The defender's arm was away from the body, making the body unnaturally bigger, and should have resulted in a penalty kick.

The VAR protocol includes 'penalty/no penalty' as a reviewable category. The protocol states the VAR should recommend an on-field review for 'clear and obvious errors.' Many experts argued that failing to penalise this handball met the threshold of a clear and obvious error and should have prompted a review.

varOutcome: VAR did not intervene — decision of no penalty was upheld despite widespread criticism.
